The assembly: (1) use PgBouncer or RDS Proxy in front of Aurora to pool database connections — vLLM workers generate many write bursts when parallel requests complete simultaneously; (2) write conversation turns asynchronously after returning the response to the user; (3) use Aurora Serverless v2 with auto-scaling for cost efficiency during low-traffic windows; (4) partition conversation tables by user_id to distribute load; (5) implement a TTL-based archival strategy (conversations older than 30 days move to S3). The write amplification from saving full conversation histories grows quadratically with conversation length — implement conversation summarization to cap context token count at 4K tokens per turn.

vLLM's use of Python asyncio rather than multi-threading for the server event loop is motivated by Python's GIL constraint. In CPython, only one thread can execute Python bytecode at a time — a multi-threaded HTTP server handling 100 concurrent connections would have 99 threads waiting for the GIL at any moment. Asyncio's event-driven model runs a single thread that switches between coroutines at I/O boundaries, avoiding GIL contention for I/O-bound operations (HTTP handling, queue management). The GPU execution (via CUDA) releases the GIL and runs independently — asyncio's coroutines handle scheduling logic while CUDA kernels execute in parallel. The limitation: CPU-bound Python code (complex JSON serialization, tokenization) blocks the asyncio event loop. For high-throughput deployments, offload tokenization to a separate process pool to prevent Python CPU work from blocking the event loop.

vLLM's checkpoint format for saving/loading in-progress experiments (not the same as model weights) is not persistent — LLMEngine state (KV cache contents, in-flight request queues) is entirely in-memory and cannot be checkpointed. Killing a vLLM process abandons all in-flight requests without any possibility of recovery. For production resilience, this means: (1) never rely on in-flight requests surviving a pod restart; (2) implement request retry logic in all clients; (3) design generation tasks to be idempotent (safe to retry from the start); (4) avoid very long single-request generations that represent significant compute investment if lost. For research tasks requiring fault tolerance on multi-hour generation jobs, split into smaller independent sub-tasks that can each complete within a reasonable failure window.

vLLM's end-to-end quality validation for a newly deployed model uses a golden set evaluation approach. The procedure: (1) curate a set of 200 'golden' prompts with reference responses that represent the primary use cases (50 for each major task type); (2) generate responses from the new model at temperature=0 with the same SamplingParams used in production; (3) compute automated metrics: BLEU for summarization tasks, CodeBLEU for code generation, exact match for classification, G-Eval or LLM-as-judge for open-ended tasks; (4) compare against the golden reference set stored from the previous model version; (5) flag any task type where the new model scores >5% below the previous model. This validation catches silent capability regressions that benchmark metrics like MMLU don't capture because they don't reflect production task distribution.

vLLM's production serving for real-time voice AI (text-to-speech pipeline integration) requires streaming tokens to a TTS model with minimal TTFT. The assembly: (1) use vLLM streaming with sentence-boundary detection — accumulate tokens until a sentence terminator (., !, ?) is detected; (2) submit the complete sentence to the TTS model while vLLM continues generating the next sentence; (3) queue the TTS audio outputs and begin playback of the first sentence before the second is complete. This pipelining hides the TTS generation latency behind LLM generation. The critical timing: the first sentence should be 15-30 words (1-3 seconds of audio) to balance minimizing TTFT for first audio byte vs. providing enough context for natural TTS intonation. Shorter sentences produce faster TTFT but more robotic TTS outputs due to limited intonation context.

vLLM's tensor parallel size must be a divisor of the model's num_attention_heads and intermediate_size (FFN layer). For Llama-3.1-8B (32 attention heads, 14336 FFN intermediate): tensor_parallel_size can be 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32 (divisors of 32); setting tensor_parallel_size=3 or 5 is incompatible and vLLM will raise a ValueError at startup. For Llama-3.1-70B (64 attention heads): tensor_parallel_size of 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64 are valid. The constraint also applies to KV heads — for GQA models with 8 KV heads (Llama-3 8B and 70B), tensor_parallel_size cannot exceed 8 because KV heads cannot be further divided. Using tensor_parallel_size=16 on an 8-KV-head model fails at startup with an incompatible parallelism error.

vLLM's batch evaluation pipeline for RLHF reward model scoring uses vLLM to generate multiple candidate completions per prompt efficiently. The assembly for RLHF data collection: (1) load prompts from the dataset; (2) use LLM.generate(prompts, n=4, temperature=0.8, max_tokens=512) to generate 4 completions per prompt in a single batch call; (3) pass all completions to the reward model for scoring; (4) store (prompt, completion, reward) tuples for PPO/DPO training. The efficiency advantage: n=4 with prefix cache means the prompt KV cache is computed once and shared across all 4 completions — 4x completions at ~1.5x the compute cost of 1 completion. For large-scale RLHF data collection (millions of prompts), this batching pattern reduces GPU hours by 40-60% compared to generating completions one at a time.

vLLM's structured output with outlines-based guided decoding fails for JSON schemas containing format validators (e.g., 'format': 'email', 'format': 'date-time'). The outlines FSM only validates structural JSON constraints (required fields, types, enum values) but ignores semantic format constraints like 'email' or 'date'. A schema specifying email format accepts any string as a valid email, including obviously invalid strings like 'not-an-email'. Applications that rely on guided decoding to enforce semantic format correctness will silently receive invalid values. The fix: always post-process and validate format-constrained fields after receiving the guided output (use email-validator, dateutil.parse, etc.), even when using guided decoding. Treat guided decoding as a structural validator, not a semantic validator.

vLLM's continuous delivery of improvements (weekly minor releases) requires a discipline of pinned dependencies in production combined with regular validation-gated upgrades. The rationale: each release can improve throughput 5-15% through scheduler optimizations, fix memory leaks, or add new model support — but can also introduce regressions. The recommended upgrade cadence: quarterly minor version upgrades with a 2-week validation period per upgrade. The validation process: run the golden set evaluation (brick vllm-l2-195), run the benchmark suite (brick vllm-l2-023), and run the pre-deploy validation script (brick vllm-l2-180). Only if all pass should the upgrade proceed to production. Emergency security patches override this process but require expedited validation. The quarterly upgrade cadence keeps the production deployment within 2-3 minor versions of HEAD, preventing the tech debt accumulation that makes major version upgrades disruptive.
